summary: Gruber reviews both iPhone 6 models after a week of testing, concluding that the 4.7-inch iPhone 6 feels like a natural evolution of the iPhone while the 5.5-inch iPhone 6 Plus is essentially a new device class — a hybrid between iPhone and iPad Mini. He personally prefers the smaller model despite acknowledging the Plus's superior display, camera stabilization, and battery life. The review covers design, display quality (including the Plus's novel downsampling approach), one-handed usability tradeoffs, and pocketability concerns. He criticizes Apple's decision to keep the entry-level storage at 16 GB as the most disappointing aspect of the new phones. Overall, he finds the improvements genuinely impressive but notes that Apple's consistent excellence has made such advances feel almost routine.
key_points: The iPhone 6 and 6 Plus are effectively two different device classes rather than two sizes of the same phone — the Plus wants to replace your iPad and possibly your MacBook
The 6 Plus's on-the-fly downsampling from virtual 2208×1242 to physical 1920×1080 works surprisingly well despite sounding technically dubious
Larger screens represent a genuine design tradeoff: worse one-handed use but better two-handed typing and reading, with no single size pleasing everyone
The 16 GB entry-level storage tier is indefensible when the phone is 50 times faster than the original iPhone but has only twice the storage
Apple's sustained year-over-year improvement has become so consistent that genuinely excellent products now feel routine
money_quotes: If you want to leave the house — or at least just leave your desk — with just one computer, the iPhone 6 Plus is it.
The increased size of the iPhone 6 makes it worse when using it one-handed. But it makes it better when using it two-handed.
I'd rather have an iPhone that couldn't make phone calls than one that couldn't take photographs.
The entry-level iPhones 6 are 50 times faster than that original iPhone, but have only twice the storage capacity. That's just wrong.
The most amazing thing about the iPhone 6 and 6 Plus is how utterly un-amazing it now seems to see Apple pull off this level of year-over-year improvement year after year after year.
When people see the iPhone 6 Plus in the flesh, their opinions are polarized. Either 'Wow, that's huge. I would never want a phone that big,' or, 'Wow, that's huge. I can't wait to get one of those.'
key_insight: The iPhone 6 and 6 Plus represent not just two sizes but two fundamentally different device philosophies, and Apple's real achievement is making the tradeoffs between them so clear that choosing is easy.
